subject: professional_responsibility
fact_pattern: Lawyer Mona receives a $10,000 advance from a client on Monday to be applied toward fees in an ongoing litigation matter. The fees have not yet been earned. Mona is busy and does not deposit the funds into her trust account until the following Thursday — four business days later.
call_of_question: Has Mona complied with her trust accounting obligations under By-Law 9 regarding the timing of deposit of client funds?

options.A: Yes — a lawyer has a full business week to deposit client funds into trust.
options.B: No — By-Law 9 requires that funds received in trust be deposited to the mixed trust account no later than the close of business on the next banking day following receipt.
options.C: Yes — the deposit timing rules under By-Law 9 apply only to real estate transaction funds, not to litigation retainers.
options.D: No — By-Law 9 requires that all client funds be deposited to trust within three business days; Mona deposited on the fourth business day, which is late.

explanation: By-Law 9 requires that funds received in trust be deposited to the lawyer's mixed trust account no later than the close of business on the next banking day after they are received. Mona received the funds on Monday but did not deposit until Thursday — four business days later — which clearly violates the prompt deposit requirement. The rule applies to all unearned client funds, not only real estate transactions.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. A full business week is not the standard. By-Law 9 requires deposit by the close of business on the next banking day after receipt.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — By-Law 9 requires that funds received in trust be deposited to the mixed trust account no later than the close of business on the next banking day following receipt.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The deposit timing rules apply to all client funds received in trust, not only real estate transactions.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. While Mona's deposit is indeed late, the standard is the next banking day, not three business days. Option D states an incorrect time limit.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Trust Accounting
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: LSO By-Law 9 (Financial Transactions and Records of Licensees), s. 3

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: trust accounting — mixed trust deposit timing

tested_concepts: By-Law 9 — deposit to trust by next banking day
prompt deposit requirement applies to all client funds
unearned funds must go to trust

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Specific Trust Account — Identified Client Funds
fact_pattern: Lawyer Noel is acting for the purchaser in a commercial real estate transaction. The purchase price is $4,000,000. The seller insists that the deposit funds be held in a separate interest-bearing account exclusively for this transaction rather than in Noel's general mixed trust account. Noel is considering his options.
call_of_question: Which of the following accurately describes the use and purpose of a specific trust account under By-Law 9?

options.A: A specific trust account is prohibited — all client funds must be pooled in a single mixed trust account.
options.B: A specific trust account is an account opened and maintained for the funds of a specific client or a specific transaction, separately from the mixed trust account; it is typically used where a client instructs the lawyer to hold funds in a separate interest-bearing account or where the nature of the transaction requires segregation of funds.
options.C: A specific trust account may only be opened where the Law Society of Ontario has approved it for the specific transaction.
options.D: A specific trust account operates identically to a mixed trust account and provides no separate identification of the funds belonging to a particular client.

explanation: By-Law 9 contemplates both mixed trust accounts (where funds of multiple clients are pooled) and specific trust accounts (also called designated trust accounts) where funds belonging to a specific client or transaction are held separately. A specific trust account is appropriate for large transactions or where the client requests or requires that funds be held in a separate interest-bearing account. The interest earned in a specific trust account belongs to the client.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. By-Law 9 expressly contemplates specific (designated) trust accounts in addition to mixed trust accounts. Lawyers may open specific trust accounts for individual clients or transactions.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A specific trust account is an account opened and maintained for the funds of a specific client or a specific transaction, separately from the mixed trust account; it is typically used where a client instructs the lawyer to hold funds in a separate interest-bearing account or where the nature of the transaction requires segregation of funds.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. LSO pre-approval is not required before opening a specific trust account. The lawyer may open one as appropriate to the client's instructions or the nature of the matter.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. A specific trust account is distinguished from a mixed trust account precisely because it holds funds for one client or transaction only, providing clear identification and segregation.
tested_concepts: By-Law 9 — specific trust vs. mixed trust account
specific trust for identified client funds
interest in specific trust belongs to client

subject: professional_responsibility
fact_pattern: Lawyer Oliver has been running a busy real estate practice for two years. He maintains a mixed trust account and keeps a trust ledger, a client trust listing, and receives monthly bank statements. Oliver reconciles his bank statement to his trust ledger on an ad hoc basis — roughly every three or four months — when he has time. He believes this frequency is adequate because he has never had a shortfall.
call_of_question: Has Oliver satisfied his trust reconciliation obligations under By-Law 9?

options.A: Yes — trust reconciliation is required only annually for lawyers with a real estate practice.
options.B: No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer perform a three-way reconciliation of the trust bank statement, the trust ledger, and the client trust listing on a monthly basis; Oliver's ad hoc reconciliation every three to four months is non-compliant regardless of whether a shortfall exists.
options.C: Yes — the absence of a shortfall demonstrates that the accounts are in order, which satisfies the reconciliation requirement.
options.D: No — By-Law 9 requires reconciliation within 10 business days after each transaction, not on a monthly basis.

explanation: By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer perform a three-way reconciliation at least monthly: the trust bank statement balance must be reconciled to the trust ledger balance, which must in turn reconcile to the total of the client trust listing (the sum of individual client balances). Monthly reconciliation is a mandatory compliance obligation, not a best practice. The absence of a known shortfall does not substitute for performing the reconciliation — discrepancies may exist without the lawyer being aware of them.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Trust reconciliation is required monthly, not annually. The frequency of reconciliation is a firm requirement under By-Law 9.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer perform a three-way reconciliation of the trust bank statement, the trust ledger, and the client trust listing on a monthly basis; Oliver's ad hoc reconciliation every three to four months is non-compliant regardless of whether a shortfall exists.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The absence of a known shortfall does not satisfy the reconciliation requirement. Reconciliation is required to detect discrepancies, not merely to confirm known problems.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. By-Law 9 requires monthly reconciliation, not transaction-by-transaction reconciliation within 10 days. The monthly requirement is tied to the bank statement cycle.
tested_concepts: By-Law 9 — monthly three-way trust reconciliation
reconciliation is mandatory not optional
absence of shortfall does not substitute for reconciliation

subject: professional_responsibility
fact_pattern: Lawyer Petra has completed a real estate transaction and holds $8,500 in trust that represents funds owed to her for fees and disbursements. She has rendered a final account to the client. Rather than waiting for the client to provide written direction, Petra transfers the $8,500 from trust to her general account because she has already rendered the final account and believes the amount is undisputed.
call_of_question: Has Petra complied with the requirements for withdrawing funds from trust under By-Law 9?

options.A: Yes — once a final account has been rendered, the lawyer is entitled to transfer trust funds to the general account without any further formality.
options.B: No — trust funds may only be withdrawn by a lawyer if the client first provides a written direction authorizing the transfer; rendering a final account alone does not authorize withdrawal.
options.C: Yes — rendering a final account is the functional equivalent of a written direction for trust withdrawal purposes.
options.D: No — trust funds may never be transferred to a lawyer's general account for fee payment; fees can only be paid in cash by the client.

explanation: By-Law 9 establishes strict requirements for withdrawing funds from trust. A lawyer may withdraw funds from trust only in accordance with certain permitted grounds, which include having a written direction from the client, a court order, or other authorization. Rendering a final account creates an entitlement to be paid but does not itself constitute authorization to take money from trust. Petra must have a written direction from the client or another valid basis before transferring trust funds to her general account.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Rendering a final account does not automatically authorize a trust withdrawal. By-Law 9 requires a written direction or other valid ground before funds can be transferred from trust.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — trust funds may only be withdrawn by a lawyer if the client first provides a written direction authorizing the transfer; rendering a final account alone does not authorize withdrawal.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. A rendered account and a written direction are distinct requirements. The account identifies the amount owed; the written direction authorizes the transfer of trust funds.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Trust funds may indeed be transferred to a general account for fee payment — the issue is the authorization process, not whether such transfers are ever permissible.
tested_concepts: By-Law 9 — written direction required for trust withdrawal
rendered account does not authorize withdrawal
authorization requirements for trust transfers

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Trust Record-Keeping — Required Books and Retention Period
fact_pattern: Lawyer Quinn closed her practice four years ago. She retained all her client files and trust records for three years after closing and then destroyed them to free up storage space. A former client now contacts her requesting copies of trust records related to a real estate transaction from five years ago. Quinn informs the client that all records have been destroyed.
call_of_question: Has Quinn satisfied her trust record-keeping retention obligations under By-Law 9?

options.A: Yes — a three-year retention period for trust records is adequate under By-Law 9.
options.B: Yes — lawyers who close their practice are exempt from ongoing record-keeping obligations.
options.C: No — By-Law 9 requires that trust records, including books of account and supporting documentation, be retained for a minimum of six years; Quinn destroyed records after three years and failed to meet this requirement.
options.D: No — trust records must be retained permanently and may never be destroyed.

explanation: By-Law 9 requires that lawyers retain their books of account, including trust records, for a minimum of six years from the end of the fiscal year to which they relate. Quinn retained records for only three years before destroying them, which does not satisfy the six-year minimum. The obligation to retain records for six years applies even after a lawyer closes their practice. The records from a transaction five years ago should still have been available.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Three years is less than the six-year minimum required by By-Law 9. Quinn's retention period was insufficient.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. Closing a practice does not exempt a lawyer from the obligation to retain trust records for the required period. The six-year retention requirement continues after the practice closes.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — By-Law 9 requires that trust records, including books of account and supporting documentation, be retained for a minimum of six years; Quinn destroyed records after three years and failed to meet this requirement.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Trust records need not be retained permanently — six years is the required minimum. After six years, records may be destroyed.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Trust Accounting
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: LSO By-Law 9, s. 18 (record retention — six years)

tested_concepts: By-Law 9 — six-year trust record retention minimum
retention obligation survives practice closure
three years is insufficient

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Client Trust Ledger — Required Contents and Per-Client Accounting
fact_pattern: Lawyer Renata operates a busy practice and maintains a mixed trust account for 40 active clients. She tracks all trust money received in a single general ledger entry for the mixed trust account, showing total deposits and withdrawals chronologically, but does not maintain a separate ledger entry for each individual client. When asked by the LSO during a compliance audit what balance is held in trust for a specific client, Renata cannot produce that information without extensive manual calculation.
call_of_question: Has Renata complied with the trust ledger requirements of By-Law 9?

options.A: Yes — a single combined trust account ledger is sufficient under By-Law 9 as long as the total trust balance is accurately tracked.
options.B: No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer maintain a separate trust ledger for each client whose funds are held in the mixed trust account; the ledger must show all trust transactions for that specific client and must allow the lawyer to identify the balance held for each client at any time.
options.C: Yes — the requirement for a per-client trust ledger only applies to lawyers handling real estate or estate matters; litigation trusts may be tracked in aggregate.
options.D: No — but the per-client ledger is only required if the trust account holds funds for more than 20 clients simultaneously.

explanation: By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer maintain a separate trust ledger account for each client or matter for which trust funds are held. The per-client trust ledger must record all receipts and disbursements of trust funds for that client and must be maintained in a way that allows the lawyer to identify the balance held for each client at any time. A single combined chronological entry for all clients is non-compliant — it makes it impossible to determine individual client balances without extensive manual reconstruction, which is precisely the problem By-Law 9's record-keeping requirements are designed to prevent.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. A combined trust account ledger is insufficient. By-Law 9 requires per-client ledger entries so that each client's balance is separately and readily identifiable at all times.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer maintain a separate trust ledger for each client whose funds are held in the mixed trust account; the ledger must show all trust transactions for that specific client and must allow the lawyer to identify the balance held for each client at any time.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The per-client trust ledger requirement applies to all areas of practice, not only real estate or estate matters. Any client funds held in a mixed trust account must be tracked with a separate per-client ledger.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. There is no threshold number of clients above which per-client ledgers are required. The requirement applies from the moment a lawyer holds trust funds for any client.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Trust Accounting
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: LSO By-Law 9, s. 8 (client trust ledger — per-client accounting)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: trust accounting — client trust ledger requirements

tested_concepts: By-Law 9 — separate trust ledger required for each client
per-client ledger must show individual balance at any time
combined chronological ledger is non-compliant

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Shortage in Trust Account — Obligations on Discovery
fact_pattern: During a monthly trust reconciliation, Lawyer Seamus discovers that his trust account has a $15,000 shortage — the total shown on the client trust listing exceeds the bank balance by $15,000. Seamus cannot immediately identify the cause of the shortage. He decides to investigate quietly for 30 days before taking any further steps, hoping to identify and remedy the shortage before it becomes a formal problem.
call_of_question: What are Seamus's obligations upon discovering a shortage in his trust account under By-Law 9 and the Rules of Professional Conduct?

options.A: Seamus may investigate the shortage quietly for up to 90 days before any reporting obligation is triggered.
options.B: Seamus must immediately remedy the shortage from his personal funds and then investigate; reporting to the LSO is only required if he cannot restore the shortage within 30 days.
options.C: Upon discovering a trust shortage, Seamus must take immediate steps to identify and remedy the shortage, ensure no further client funds are at risk, and report the shortage to the LSO; a trust shortage is a serious matter requiring prompt action and cannot be managed quietly for an extended period before the LSO is notified.
options.D: Seamus may self-report to the LSO within 60 days of discovery; no earlier reporting is required as long as active investigation is underway.

explanation: By-Law 9 and the LSO's requirements on trust shortages require that a lawyer who discovers a trust shortage take immediate steps to investigate, remedy the shortage, and report to the LSO. Trust funds belong to clients, not to the lawyer, and any shortage puts client interests at immediate risk. The LSO must be notified promptly — the lawyer cannot manage the situation privately while delaying notification to the regulator. A trust shortage is treated as a serious matter that may constitute professional misconduct and warrants immediate regulatory attention.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. A 90-day investigation period before reporting is not permissible. Trust shortages require immediate action, including prompt notification to the LSO.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. While remedying the shortage from personal funds is a step that should be taken, this does not replace or delay the reporting obligation to the LSO. Reporting is required regardless of whether personal funds can remedy the shortage.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Upon discovering a trust shortage, Seamus must take immediate steps to identify and remedy the shortage, ensure no further client funds are at risk, and report the shortage to the LSO; a trust shortage is a serious matter requiring prompt action and cannot be managed quietly for an extended period before the LSO is notified.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. A 60-day self-reporting window is not established under By-Law 9. Shortages must be reported promptly — not within a fixed self-selected period of investigation.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Trust Accounting
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: LSO By-Law 9 (trust shortage reporting obligations); Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: trust accounting — shortage in trust account — reporting obligations

tested_concepts: trust shortage — immediate remediation and LSO reporting required
cannot manage trust shortage quietly without reporting
trust shortage is serious professional responsibility matter

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Law Society Audit of Trust Accounts — Lawyer's Obligations
fact_pattern: The Law Society of Ontario notifies Lawyer Tamara that an inspector will attend her office to conduct a trust account audit under the Law Society Act. Tamara consults with a colleague who tells her she may refuse to allow the inspector access because the audit would interfere with solicitor-client privilege over her client files. Tamara is considering whether to allow the inspection.
call_of_question: Is Tamara entitled to refuse the LSO inspector access to her trust account records on the basis of solicitor-client privilege?

options.A: Yes — solicitor-client privilege absolutely protects all files in a law office from LSO inspection.
options.B: Yes — Tamara may refuse access until a court order specifically authorizing the audit is obtained.
options.C: No — the Law Society Act grants LSO inspectors the authority to inspect trust accounts and financial records; while solicitor-client privilege may apply to protect the substance of client communications, it does not shield trust account records and financial books from LSO inspection; Tamara must cooperate with the audit.
options.D: No — but Tamara may require the inspector to sign a confidentiality agreement before access is granted.

explanation: The Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8 grants Law Society inspectors the authority to inspect lawyers' books of account, financial records, and trust accounts as part of the LSO's regulatory oversight function. Solicitor-client privilege protects confidential communications between lawyer and client made for the purpose of legal advice — it does not protect trust account records, bank statements, ledgers, or financial books from regulatory inspection. Tamara must cooperate with the audit. Obstruction of a Law Society inspector is itself professional misconduct.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Solicitor-client privilege does not shield trust account records and financial books from LSO inspection. Privilege applies to the substance of client communications, not to financial records.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. A court order is not required before the LSO may conduct a trust account audit. The Law Society Act grants the authority directly and a lawyer must cooperate with an authorized inspection.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — the Law Society Act grants LSO inspectors the authority to inspect trust accounts and financial records; while solicitor-client privilege may apply to protect the substance of client communications, it does not shield trust account records and financial books from LSO inspection; Tamara must cooperate with the audit.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. An inspector conducting an authorized LSO audit is not required to sign a confidentiality agreement as a condition of access. The inspector operates under the Law Society Act, which authorizes the inspection.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Trust Accounting
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 42 (inspection and audit of financial records)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: trust accounting — LSO audit authority; solicitor-client privilege does not shield financial records

tested_concepts: Law Society Act — LSO authority to inspect trust accounts
solicitor-client privilege does not protect financial records from audit
obstruction of LSO inspector is professional misconduct

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Paying Out Trust Funds — Authority Required
fact_pattern: Lawyer Ursula settles a personal injury matter for her client for $120,000. The settlement funds are deposited to trust. The client agrees verbally on the phone that Ursula may pay herself her contingency fee of $40,000 from trust and send the remaining $80,000 to the client by wire transfer. Ursula transfers the funds in accordance with the phone call without obtaining a signed direction from the client and without providing a written statement of account.
call_of_question: Has Ursula complied with By-Law 9 requirements for paying out funds from trust?

options.A: Yes — a verbal direction from the client is sufficient authority for a lawyer to pay out trust funds.
options.B: No — By-Law 9 requires a signed written direction from the client before disbursing trust funds; a verbal direction is insufficient; Ursula should also have rendered a written statement of account before deducting her contingency fee from trust.
options.C: Yes — because the client agreed to the payment breakdown, Ursula's obligation to obtain written authorization is satisfied by the verbal agreement.
options.D: No — but Ursula's only obligation was to provide a written statement of account; she did not need a separate written direction from the client.

explanation: By-Law 9 requires that disbursements from a trust account be made only in accordance with a signed written direction from the client or another valid authorization such as a court order. A verbal direction does not satisfy this requirement. Additionally, before deducting fees from settlement trust funds, a lawyer must provide the client with a written statement of account setting out the fees and disbursements being claimed. Ursula's failure to obtain a written direction and a written account means she has not complied with By-Law 9, even if the client verbally agreed to the distribution.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. A verbal direction does not satisfy the requirement under By-Law 9. Written authorization from the client is required before trust funds may be paid out.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — By-Law 9 requires a signed written direction from the client before disbursing trust funds; a verbal direction is insufficient; Ursula should also have rendered a written statement of account before deducting her contingency fee from trust.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Verbal agreement, however genuine, does not substitute for the written direction required under By-Law 9. The written requirement is a formal safeguard, not merely a default position.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Both the written direction and the written statement of account are required before trust funds may be disbursed. The written direction is not optional simply because a written account is also required.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Trust Accounting
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: LSO By-Law 9, s. 9 (paying out trust funds — written direction required)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: trust accounting — paying out trust funds requires written direction

tested_concepts: By-Law 9 — written direction required for trust payout
verbal direction is insufficient authority for trust disbursement
written statement of account required before deducting fees from trust
